conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start by reading the official guidance on Child Benefit eligibility to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex and he's 16 years old. I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I can help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is 16 years old, his eligibility depends on whether he's in approved education or training. 

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or in an approved training program)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex recently left school a couple of months ago. He's not in education or training at the moment, but he's signed up with the local careers service to figure out what he wants to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know.

According to the rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services.

To help me assess Alex's eligibility accurately, I need to ask a few more questions about your circumstances:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I live in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex to determine if you're responsible for him.

**Do you live with Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just to confirm a few final details:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me just confirm one more important detail:

**How long ago exactly did Alex leave education?** (I need to know if it's been less than or more than 20 weeks since he left school)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about a couple of months since he left school, so well within the 20 weeks.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided, here's why you qualify:

✓ You live in the UK (Leeds)
✓ You live with Alex and are responsible for him
✓ Alex is 16 years old and recently left education
✓ **Importantly**, Alex has registered with the local careers service
✓ It's only been a couple of months since he left school (well within the 20-week allowance)
✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him

According to the rules, Child Benefit can continue for **up to 20 weeks** when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service (which Alex has done). Since only a couple of months have passed, you're still within this 20-week period.

**Important to note:** If Alex doesn't return to approved education or training within the 20-week period, your Child Benefit for him will stop. However, if he does start approved education or training again, you can continue to claim Child Benefit for him until he turns 20 (as long as he remains in approved education or training).

You can apply for or continue your Child Benefit claim through HMRC. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and left education a couple of months ago (well within 20 weeks). According to the eligibility rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex has registered with the local careers service, which meets this requirement. Additionally, the claimant lives in the UK (Leeds), lives with Alex, and is responsible for him. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex during this 20-week period after leaving education.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 16-year-old son Alex. Although Alex recently left school, he registered with the local careers service, which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks. Since only a couple of months have passed (well within the 20-week limit), the claimant meets all the eligibility criteria: they live in the UK, live with Alex, are responsible for him, and no one else is claiming for him.

Since only a couple of months have passed (well within the 20-week limit), the claimant meets all the eligibility criteria: they live in the UK, live with Alex, are responsible for him, and no one else is claiming for him.
